body_txt: Alice tells Bob to shut up and he hasn't even said anything. However, she just knows that Bob's about to offer an opinion—or at least that he would be justified in doing so. This is often used to lampshade Hypocritical Humor or Blatant Lies , or to forestall the obvious answer when Alice has walked into a Double Entendre or Rhetorical Question Blunder . Similar stock responses include " Don't Ask ," "Don't say it," "Don't say a word," and "Not one word." For extra humor, the person being told to shut up is occasionally He Who Must Not Be Heard . Compare Inner Monologue Conversation . When it's a lawyer saying this to a client, it's Don't Answer That . It can be an effective source of Rapid-Fire Interrupting . See also Preemptive Declaration and Rapid-Fire "Shut Up!" . Contrast Shutting Up Now .
